what_it_does: This file is a build specification that defines a pure-function resolver architecture—resolve(invariant, coordinate) → concrete surface—for computing surfaces from one invariant definition evaluated against a multi-axis context coordinate, and it assigns implementation lanes and a prove-on-one sequencing plan around a decision-card keystone.
observations: Declares the invariant law: never build a variant; every concrete surface is computed on demand from one invariant plus a coordinate.

Lists orthogonal context axes including device/medium, viewer/principal, mode, perspective, type, intent, resolution, state/time, posture/permission, register/theme, and later adds preference/accessibility.
Distinguishes continuous axes (resolved by derivation/function of a root) from discrete/categorical axes (resolved by registry selection).
Identifies four primitives: tokens, types/archetypes, content, and rules-computation as the missing verb.
Argues the resolver is not UI-scoped: the invariant is the whole company, and UI/tools/cognition are projections of it.
Frames the resolver as a recovery of Tim's prior formal constructs: universal-evaluator, coupled-coalgebra-runtime, monodromy-structure, eigenpoint-lattice.
Reconciles content vs host: content resolves from the type axis, while host allocation/layout must be computed from the device axis, not hand-written breakpoints.
Assigns lanes—composition, DNA, projection, fork, lead—with specific responsibilities for the resolver build.
Emphasizes reuse of existing seeds: dials, rule tool, project/projection instrument, tokens, slot_map.
Includes project-management notes such as overnight reality, fork availability, and security-critical gating for D3.
standouts: The resolver is presented as Tim's already-formalized theorem (universal-evaluator) recovered, not a new invention.
Device-type is treated as merely one axis of one projection; the real invariant spans the whole company.
Resolution is operations-fractal: one resolver applied at app, screen, card, slot, and token scales.
The 'relationships not cases' law is split into two mechanisms: continuous derivation and discrete registry selection.
The entire plan is anchored to a prove-on-one keystone: finish the decision card first, then resolve it across devices.
conventions: Follows markdown heading/bullet structure with timestamped notes and explicit 'offered, not asserted' hedging.
Uses star markers (★, ★★) to flag emphasis internally despite the no-ranking instruction.
Mixes design specification with project-management status, lane assignments, and personnel gating.
Employs dense neologisms and capitalized proper nouns for concepts (Resolver, Invariant, Coordinate, Registry).
concerns: Heavy reliance on external formal vaults/theorem constructs that are not reproduced or verified inside this file.
The exact axis set is explicitly provisional, creating implementation ambiguity until root axes converge.
Multiple lane owners may create integration seams if the resolver contract is not finalized before parallel work.
The claim of a 'pure function' resolver coexists with live/stateful axes (mode, state/time) without detailed operational reconciliation.
Cycle-close work is gated on a single security-critical owner (fork), creating a bottleneck risk.
notes: A build spec that turns a 'one invariant, many contexts' design philosophy into a sequenced engineering plan centered on a pure resolver function and a multi-axis coordinate model.
questions: What is the exact schema of a coordinate and how is it supplied at runtime?
How does the resolver resolve conflicts when multiple axes select incompatible render families?
What is the concrete API signature and data structure of resolve(invariant, coordinate)?
How are dials, rule, and project/projection technically unified into a single engine?
What does 'at-bar' mean as a completion criterion?
Which files contain the referenced theorem sources and prior formalization?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: it frames the larger invariant-company resolver and supplies buildable lanes and sequencing, though much of the 'buildable' detail remains at the contract level rather than the code level.
apparent_themes: Orthogonal multi-axis context coordinates with no ranking
Self-covering systems that judge their own computed surfaces
Formal grounding in prior theorem work
intention_signals: Consolidate a sprawling design conversation into an actionable, buildable specification for a context-aware resolver that computes every surface from one invariant definition, while keeping the team aligned to a single keystone proof and Tim's existing formal framework.
novelty: Treats the entire company as an invariant resolved against a multi-dimensional coordinate, with device/UI as merely one projection; explicitly distinguishes continuous derivation from discrete registry-selection as two resolution mechanisms under one 'no variants' law.
contribution: A structured vocabulary for invariant resolution (axes, axis-kinds, primitives, resolver contract), a sequenced prove-on-one plan anchored on a decision-card keystone, and a reuse-oriented mapping of existing seeds into a unified resolver primitive.
summary_for_embedding: RESOLVER-BUILD.md is a build specification for a pure-function resolve(invariant, coordinate) system that computes concrete company surfaces from a single invariant definition evaluated against orthogonal context axes, with lane assignments and prove-on-one sequencing anchored on a decision-card keystone.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to make the 'invariant application' concept buildable by defining the resolver contract, surfacing the larger company-wide scope, and sequencing implementation across composition, DNA, projection, fork, and lead lanes.
suggested_fields: formal_source_vault mapping each construct to its theorem source
